Should You Buy This Property?

Property: 759 Boulder Ct, Stateline, NV 89449  |  Your share if total loss: $334,000  |  Analysis date: 2026-06-19
This memo replaces the earlier "self-insure vs insure" analysis. Two corrections changed the numbers materially: (1) I rebuilt the interior-fire risk after auditing the underlying NFPA data — my first pass overstated it ~6×; (2) I cross-checked the wildfire risk against 41 years of actual fire history within 50 miles of the parcel, which revealed that the official USFS model dramatically understates real risk for this area.
The honest read: this is a high-wildfire-risk parcel
Empirical fire history within 50 miles of 759 Boulder Ct since 1984 shows fires in 40 of 41 years, including fires within 0.6 miles (Gondola 2002), 1.4 miles (Autumn Hills 1996), 6 miles (Caldor 2021, which destroyed ~1,000 structures), and 7.9 miles (Angora 2007, which destroyed 254 homes).

Over 15 years of ownership, the realistic probability that the building burns down is about 4.1%, with a credible range of 0.8% to 15.0%. That's roughly 1 chance in 24.

Whether to buy depends on whether you can absorb a ~1-in-24 chance of losing $334K over 15 years — on top of the property's other costs and the rising insurance unavailability.

The fire history map — what really happens around this parcel

Every red/orange shape is a fire that burned ≥100 acres within 20 miles of the parcel since 1984. Darker red = more recent. The Gondola fire (2002, 643 acres) burned within 0.6 miles; Autumn Hills (1996, 3,805 acres) within 1.4 miles; Caldor (2021, 221,786 acres) within 6 miles and destroyed ~1,000 structures in Grizzly Flats; Angora (2007, 3,070 acres) within 7.9 miles and destroyed 254 homes in South Lake Tahoe.
Left: the 50-mile context — 394 separate fires ≥100 acres since 1984. Right: the empirical numbers — within 5 miles of the parcel, a fire has occurred in 7% of years; within 10 miles, 17%; within 20 miles, 63%; within 30 miles, 85%. The blue line shows what the USFS Wildfire Risk to Communities model said for the parcel: 0.04% per year. The model is roughly 150× lower than what history actually shows for fires within 10 miles.
Why the official US model says "low risk" when fires happen here all the time. The WRC FSim model is a 10,000-year stochastic simulation using LANDFIRE 2020 fuel data and historical weather. Three things make it understate risk for this parcel: (1) the parcel itself sits on a "developed" cell with no fuel, so its fire-spread potential is filled in by averaging surrounding cells; (2) it predates Caldor 2021 and the recent Sierra fire escalation; (3) it doesn't model human-caused ignitions (powerlines, vehicles, vegetation management) that drive most WUI fires. The empirical history is the better anchor.

What the corrected model says about the next 15 years

Each year you own the property, the chance the building has burned down by then grows. After 5 years: about 1.2%. After 10 years: 2.6%. After 15 years: 4.1%. The shaded band shows the uncertainty range — the lower end assumes the empirical wildfire risk is overstated; the upper end assumes Caldor-class events keep happening at the recent rate. The middle line is the most likely scenario.
Imagine running the next 15 years 10,000 times. About 9,592 times the building is fine and you walk away. About 407 times the building burns down and you lose your $334,000 share. The question is whether you can afford to be one of the 407.

Why the wildfire risk is so much higher than the first model said

The blue bar is what the official US model said: 0.04% chance of fire reaching this parcel per year. The orange bar is the actual empirical rate of fires within 5 miles of the parcel: 7% per year (5 fires in 41 years). The red bar is for fires within 10 miles: 17% per year. The model and empirical reality disagree by about 150×. We used the empirical rate, not the model, for the corrected analysis.

What this means for your buying decision

The case AGAINST buying

  • Real wildfire risk to the building is meaningful — somewhere between 1% and 15% chance of total loss over 15 years, most likely ~4%. That's not "tail risk" — it's a real probability.
  • Insurance is essentially unavailable — the HOA already voted down a $24K/year policy because it was prohibitive. If you buy, you're absorbing the full $334K downside yourself.
  • Resale risk: insurance unavailability propagates to value — buyers in 5–10 years may face even worse insurance markets. Properties in unisurable zones are starting to trade at discounts of 10–30%.
  • Climate trend is unfavorable — the Tahoe basin has had 4 major fires in the last 18 years (Angora 2007, multiple smaller, Caldor 2021, Tamarack 2021). Trend lines aren't improving.
  • You're inheriting other people's risk decisions — the dual-HOA structure means defensible space and structural mitigation depend on HOA decisions you don't control.

The case FOR buying

  • The building has hardened mitigation — retired-firefighter manager, sprinkler systems, maintained defensible space. Building's actual destruction rate is likely lower than average for the area.
  • Tahoe-area real estate has resilient long-term value for vacation/STR markets even with fire risk priced in.
  • Most likely outcome (~96% of scenarios over 15 years) is no total loss. The expected dollar loss is $17.6K — small compared to typical property purchase prices.
  • If purchase price is below market value by enough to compensate for the uninsured-shell risk, the math could still work.

The pragmatic test

  1. Could your household absorb a $334K loss in one year without serious financial harm?
    If NO: the risk-adjusted purchase requires a substantial price discount (think 10–25% below otherwise-comparable insured properties) to compensate. Without that discount, walk away.
    If YES: the math is more manageable. Average loss of $17.6K over 15 years is similar to other property-ownership costs (HOA fees, taxes, maintenance) — it just shows up as a low-probability lump.
  2. Is the purchase price already discounted for the wildfire risk? Compare against Tahoe properties with insurance available. If this unit is selling at parity, it's overpriced for the risk you're taking.
  3. How is your STR revenue projection affected if you're un-insurable? Standard STR loans require insurance. If you're paying cash and self-insuring, your exit options narrow.

Honest limits of this analysis

  1. The empirical wildfire rate has wide uncertainty. 5 fires within 5 miles over 41 years is a small sample. The true annual rate could be 3% or 12% depending on which fires you count and how you define "near."
  2. P(building destroyed | fire near) is the biggest model uncertainty. We use 10–30% — the range from DINS data for multi-residence buildings with hardening. Could be higher in extreme fire weather (Caldor saw >90% destruction in Grizzly Flats).
  3. Interior-fire risk is now based on NFPA national multi-family fire statistics rather than my original Beta(2,26) which was unjustifiably high. The corrected number is grounded but still uncertain ±50%.
  4. Climate trajectory uses CMIP5 (slightly dated). CMIP6 projections suggest somewhat worse warming; we're likely on the conservative side.
  5. Resale/value risk isn't modeled here. If insurance markets in the Tahoe basin deteriorate further, your exit price could drop independently of any fire occurring.

If you do buy — what to track

  • Annual fire-perimeter activity in El Dorado/Douglas County (NIFC public data) — re-evaluate if you see a Caldor-class event within 20 miles.
  • HOA budget and defensible-space maintenance — if these lapse, your risk rises.
  • Insurance market for HOA master policies — if a real carrier re-opens at <$15K/year per owner, reconsider buying coverage.
  • Building manager's tenure — when they retire, ask the HOA who's taking over the fire-readiness program.
Sources: USFS Wildfire Risk to Communities (FSim BP/FLEP), NIFC Interagency Fire Perimeter History 1984-2025, CAL FIRE DINS (132,522 structure-outcome records), NFPA US residential fire statistics, Cal-Adapt LOCA CMIP5 climate ensemble (8 models). Decision-support only; consult licensed financial/real-estate advisors before action.
